urn:li:activity:7475118134287556608.drafted_comment: The shift from "code to execute" to "prompt to orchestrate" is interesting, but I think we're underestimating how much deeper understanding still matters. When your BEV transformer hallucinates phantom objects or your agentic system gets stuck in degenerate loops, you can't prompt your way out without knowing why the failure modes exist. The real question is whether prompt engineering becomes a new kind of systems thinking or just a brittle interface layer that breaks under distribution shift.
urn:li:activity:7475118134287556608.email_subject: [APPROVE?] Comment on post by Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, Generative AI, Data Science & Python - Scientist, Analyst
urn:li:activity:7475118134287556608.author: Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, Generative AI, Data Science & Python - Scientist, Analyst
urn:li:activity:7475118134287556608.text:
First we learned to code computers. Now we’re learning to prompt them 🤷🏽‍♂️

New things that AI Introduces:

👉 Prompt Engineering: Talking to AI in the right way to get better results.

👉 Vibe Coding: Building software by guiding AI instead of writing every line yourself.

👉 AI-Assisted Developer: A programmer who collaborates with AI to code faster.

👉 Human-in-the-Loop: AI works, humans review and approve.

👉 Hallucination: When AI confidently invents wrong information.

👉 Copilot Workflow: Using AI as a real-time assistant for work and learning.

👉 Context Window: How much information AI can remember during a conversation.

👉 Agentic AI: AI that can plan, reason, and perform tasks autonomously.

👉 Synthetic Content: AI-generated text, images, audio, video, or code.

And finally : Understanding: An optional feature in modern programming. 😄

urn:li:activity:7475118183381729281.drafted_comment: I see the analogy, but calling MCP a "nervous system" might overstate its current adoption. Most production agentic systems I've seen still rely on custom tool integration layers rather than a universal protocol. Are you seeing MCP deployed widely enough to be considered infrastructure, or is this more aspirational? Also curious how you'd fit multimodal perception into this body framework, since modern agents increasingly need vision and sensor fusion beyond just language understanding.
urn:li:activity:7475118183381729281.email_subject: [APPROVE?] Comment on post by Artificial Intelligence 🟥
urn:li:activity:7475118183381729281.author: Artificial Intelligence 🟥
urn:li:activity:7475118183381729281.text:
Nobody explained AI this clearly until now.
Stop memorizing definitions.

Start thinking in analogies. 🧠
Here's the human body framework that finally makes AI systems click 👇

1️⃣ LLM = Brain

The reasoning engine.

Learns patterns, understands language, generates intelligent responses.

Without it? Nothing thinks.
2️⃣ RAG = Brain + Books

The brain that can also read.

Retrieves relevant information from external sources at query time — producing accurate, up-to-date answers.

Without it? The brain guesses.
3️⃣ AI Agent = Brain + Hands

The brain that can also act.

Plans, decides, uses tools, APIs, and external systems to complete real-world tasks autonomously — start to finish.

Without it? The brain only talks.
4️⃣ MCP = Nervous System

The universal wiring.

Standardizes how the AI brain communicates with tools, data, and external services — so any model connects to any system through one consistent protocol.

Without it? Nothing coordinates.

The complete picture:
🧠 Thinks → 📚 Knows → 🤖 Acts → ⚡ Wires It All
LLM → RAG → Agent → MCP
This is not four separate technologies.

This is one intelligent system — built layer by layer.

Most people in 2026 are still using just Layer 1.
The professionals pulling ahead have all four working together.
Which layer are you currently building on? 👇

♻️ Share this with someone who finds AI confusing. This analogy changes everything.
.
.
.
.
.

urn:li:activity:7475118190327406592.drafted_comment: I'm curious how you think about the gap between assuming a parametric distribution and what actually happens in high-dimensional spaces. Most real-world data I've worked with (especially in perception tasks) doesn't cleanly follow any of these classical forms, and forcing a Gaussian prior can sometimes hurt more than help. Do you find mixture models or non-parametric approaches like kernel density estimation more useful when the underlying structure is genuinely unknown, or is that overthinking it for most applied work?

urn:li:activity:7475118190327406592.text:
📊 𝗨𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗯𝗮𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗗𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗯𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗙𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗼𝗳 𝗗𝗮𝘁𝗮 𝗦𝗰𝗶𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲 & 𝗠𝗮𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗲 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴

One of the most important concepts every Data Scientist, Data Analyst, and Machine Learning Engineer should understand is Probability Distributions.

Whether you're building predictive models, performing statistical analysis, detecting anomalies, or evaluating uncertainty, probability distributions help describe how data behaves in the real world.

The image below highlights some of the most widely used distributions in statistics and data science.

🔹 𝗡𝗼𝗿𝗺𝗮𝗹 𝗗𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗯𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 – Often used to model natural phenomena and measurement errors.

🔹 𝗕𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗹𝗶 & 𝗕𝗶𝗻𝗼𝗺𝗶𝗮𝗹 𝗗𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗯𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 – Ideal for modeling binary outcomes such as Success/Failure, Yes/No, or Click/No Click.

🔹 𝗣𝗼𝗶𝘀𝘀𝗼𝗻 𝗗𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗯𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 – Commonly used to model the number of events occurring within a fixed interval of time or space.

🔹 𝗣𝗼𝗶𝘀𝘀𝗼𝗻 𝗗𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗯𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 – Helps estimate waiting times between events.

🔹 𝗨𝗻𝗶𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺 𝗗𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗯𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 – Assumes all outcomes have equal probability.

🔹 𝗚𝗮𝗺𝗺𝗮 & 𝗪𝗲𝗶𝗯𝘂𝗹𝗹 𝗗𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗯𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 – Frequently applied in reliability engineering, survival analysis, and risk modeling.

🔹 𝗖𝗵𝗶-𝗦𝗾𝘂𝗮𝗿𝗲, 𝘁-𝗗𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗯𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗙-𝗗𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗯𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 – Essential for hypothesis testing and statistical inference.

🔹 𝗟𝗼𝗴-𝗡𝗼𝗿𝗺𝗮𝗹 𝗗𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗯𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 – Commonly found in financial data, income distributions, and business metrics.

🔹 𝗕𝗲𝘁𝗮 𝗗𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗯𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 – Widely used in Bayesian statistics and probability estimation.

𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝗗𝗼𝗲𝘀 𝗧𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗠𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿?

✅ Better understanding of data behavior
✅ More accurate model selection
✅ Improved feature engineering
✅ Stronger statistical reasoning
✅ Better interpretation of machine learning results

Many professionals focus directly on algorithms, but the strongest data practitioners understand the statistical foundations behind the data first.

Data Science is not just about building models—it's about understanding uncertainty, variability, and patterns hidden within data.

What probability distribution do you encounter most often in your projects?

🚀 Looking to build a career in Data Analytics, Data Science, Machine Learning, or AI?

Success isn't about learning everything—it's about learning the right skills in the right order.

Platforms like 𝗗𝗮𝘁𝗮𝗙𝗹𝗮𝗶𝗿 help learners with:

✅ Structured learning paths from Python to Generative AI
✅ Hands-on projects for real-world experience
✅ Practical case studies and problem-solving skills
✅ Coding practice and interview preparation
✅ Industry-relevant, regularly updated content

💡 In the fast-changing world of Data & AI, continuous learning and practical application are the keys to staying ahead.

Invest in skills today to create opportunities tomorrow. 🚀

𝐑𝐀𝐆 𝐬𝐭𝐨𝐩𝐬 𝐛𝐞𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐚𝐧 𝐀𝐈 𝐚𝐫𝐜𝐡𝐢𝐭𝐞𝐜𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞 𝐝𝐢𝐬𝐜𝐮𝐬𝐬𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐦𝐨𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐛𝐮𝐬𝐢𝐧𝐞𝐬𝐬 𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐤𝐞𝐡𝐨𝐥𝐝𝐞𝐫𝐬 𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐫𝐭 𝐝𝐞𝐩𝐞𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐨𝐧 𝐢𝐭𝐬 𝐚𝐧𝐬𝐰𝐞𝐫𝐬.

Over the last year, one challenge has surfaced repeatedly across enterprise AI initiatives: strong pilot results often fail to translate into production trust. The issue is rarely the model itself. More often, it is the quality of retrieval, grounding, and evaluation behind the responses.

What stands out in the most successful deployments is a shift in focus from model performance to information reliability.

1. Prioritized retrieval quality as a core design principle rather than a downstream optimization exercise.

2. Established evaluation frameworks around relevance, grounding, and answer accuracy instead of relying solely on user feedback.

3. Focused on context enrichment strategies to improve response quality in domain-specific environments.

4. Recognized that hallucinations are often symptoms of poor information architecture, not just model limitations.

5. Evaluated production scalability early, avoiding costly redesigns when moving beyond proof-of-concept environments.

Enterprise AI success is not determined by how well a model generates answers. It is determined by how confidently the business can trust those answers.

The organizations seeing meaningful value from RAG are treating retrieval, governance, and evaluation as strategic capabilities rather than technical implementation details.

This cookbook offers a useful framework for teams focused on moving AI initiatives from pilot-stage experimentation to production-scale outcomes.
: https://lnkd.in/dF3xgWeq

urn:li:activity:7475118295424098304.drafted_comment: This reminds me of the "no free lunch" theorem in ML, but applied to deployment strategy. I've seen teams at IIT Roorkee benchmark new architectures against ImageNet-pretrained ResNets and assume the delta justifies production costs, but they rarely factor in inference latency or model drift over time. For something like credit propensity, do you find that explainability constraints (SHAP values, feature importance) actually push teams back toward simpler baselines more often than the raw performance numbers would suggest?
urn:li:activity:7475118295424098304.email_subject: [APPROVE?] Comment on post by AI & Machine Learning Community (moderated)
urn:li:activity:7475118295424098304.author: AI & Machine Learning Community (moderated)
urn:li:activity:7475118295424098304.text:
The cubic cat has more value than it may seem. In physics, a simplification can provide a reasonable first answer. In data science, the equivalent is building a baseline before investing in more complex models.

That baseline can be a simple rule, a historical average, or a basic statistical model. What matters is that it works as a point of comparison.

The business question is direct: does the improvement from the advanced model justify the additional cost?

In banking, a credit propensity model should first be compared against simple rules: income, payment history, average balance, and recent behavior.

In marketing, a conversion model should be measured against a basic segmentation of active, inactive, frequent, and high-value customers.

In planning, an advanced forecast should outperform a reference built with historical sales, seasonality, and known commercial events.

Without that comparison, it is easy to confuse sophistication with impact.

With the baseline, the conversation becomes clearer: how much it improves, how much it costs, how long it takes, and how explainable it is for the business.

Building the cubic cat first helps us decide when it is worth building the real cat.

What business decision in your team would improve if it had a clear baseline first?


Follow 👉 Daniel Zaldaña to explore more about AI and data science.


#AI
#ArtificialIntelligence
#DataScience

urn:li:activity:7475118340538019844.drafted_comment: I've found that in perception tasks, especially with point clouds, classical methods like DBSCAN still outperform deep learning when you have geometric priors and limited labeled data. The semi-supervised section mentions self-training, but there's growing work on contrastive pretraining (like PointContrast) that bridges unsupervised representation learning with downstream fine-tuning more effectively. Have you seen cases where the classical taxonomy here breaks down, especially when dealing with multi-modal sensor fusion where supervision exists at different levels across modalities?

urn:li:activity:7475118340538019844.author: 🦾 Machine Learning, Artificial Intelligence, Deep Learning, Computer Vision, Robotics, Gen AI, GPT
urn:li:activity:7475118340538019844.text:
📊 𝗠𝗮𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗲 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗔𝗹𝗴𝗼𝗿𝗶𝘁𝗵𝗺𝘀 𝗘𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆 𝗗𝗮𝘁𝗮 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗳𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗦𝗵𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗱 𝗨𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗱

Machine Learning continues to be one of the most impactful technologies driving innovation across industries. While new techniques emerge rapidly, mastering the fundamentals remains essential for building reliable and scalable AI solutions.

🔹 𝗦𝘂𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝘀𝗲𝗱 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴
Used when training data contains labeled outcomes.

𝗖𝗹𝗮𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗳𝗶𝗰𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻
• Logistic Regression
• Decision Trees
• Random Forest
• Support Vector Machines (SVM)
• K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)
• Naive Bayes

𝗥𝗲𝗴𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻
• Linear Regression
• Multiple Regression
• Lasso Regression

🔹 𝗨𝗻𝘀𝘂𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝘀𝗲𝗱 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴
Used to identify hidden patterns in unlabeled data.

𝗖𝗹𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴
• K-Means
• DBSCAN

𝗗𝗶𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗥𝗲𝗱𝘂𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻
• Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
• Independent Component Analysis (ICA)

𝗔𝗻𝗼𝗺𝗮𝗹𝘆 𝗗𝗲𝘁𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻
• Isolation Forest
• Z-Score Methods

🔹 𝗦𝗲𝗺𝗶-𝗦𝘂𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝘀𝗲𝗱 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴
Combines a small amount of labeled data with a large volume of unlabeled data.

• Self-Training
• Co-Training

🔹 𝗥𝗲𝗶𝗻𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗰𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴
Enables systems to learn through rewards and interactions.

• Q-Learning
• Policy Optimization
• Model-Based Reinforcement Learning

💡 The most successful ML practitioners are not those who know the most algorithms, but those who understand when, why, and how to apply them effectively.

Which algorithm has had the biggest impact on your projects and learning journey?

urn:li:activity:7475117641855139841.drafted_comment: This framing assumes clean data is even achievable at scale, but in practice most interesting ML problems involve learning useful representations *despite* noisy labels and distribution shifts. The ImageNet dataset that powered the deep learning revolution had a 6% error rate, and foundation models today are trained on scraped web data full of inconsistencies. I wonder if the real skill isn't perfect data curation but designing architectures and training procedures robust enough to extract signal from messy, real-world sources.
urn:li:activity:7475117641855139841.email_subject: [APPROVE?] Comment on post by Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, Generative AI, Python, Data Science, Agentic AI Scientist
urn:li:activity:7475117641855139841.author: Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, Generative AI, Python, Data Science, Agentic AI Scientist
urn:li:activity:7475117641855139841.text:
𝑨𝑰 𝑾𝒐𝒏'𝒕 𝑭𝒊𝒙 𝑩𝒂𝒅 𝑫𝒂𝒕𝒂!

You can build the smartest, most advanced models in the world—but if your foundation is weak, everything on top will eventually collapse. 💥

Too many teams get caught up in the hype:

👉 "AI First" sounds exciting.

👉 "Data First" is what actually works.


Before you start chasing complex algorithms, you need to ruthlessly focus on:

🔹 Data Quality – Garbage in, garbage out.
🔹 Clear Ownership – Who is responsible for what?
🔹 Strong Governance – Setting the right rules.
🔹 Reliable Metrics – Measuring what actually matters.
🔹 Trust – Can you actually rely on your data?

The bottom line: AI is only as good as the data it learns from. Build the foundation right. Then scale.

urn:li:activity:7475117795819835392.drafted_comment: The water usage angle is important, but I think the framing misses a key technical opportunity. A lot of recent work on model distillation and quantization (like the BitNet papers from Microsoft) shows we can maintain strong performance while drastically cutting inference costs. The real question isn't just "AI uses too many resources" but why we're still deploying 175B parameter models for tasks that 7B fine-tuned versions handle just as well. Are we optimizing for benchmark leaderboards or actual deployment efficiency?

urn:li:activity:7475117795819835392.author: 🦾 Machine Learning, Artificial Intelligence, Deep Learning, Computer Vision, Robotics, Gen AI, GPT
urn:li:activity:7475117795819835392.text:
This isn't a fun fact—
it's the hidden cost of AI you never see.

Every prompt feels weightless.
But behind the screen, thousands of servers 
generate massive amounts of heat 24/7.

Keeping those systems cool requires 
enormous amounts of water and energy.

We often talk about model performance.

We rarely talk about the resources required 
to run those models at scale.

🧠 Understand the real resource 
intensity of the models we build.

The future of AI isn't just about intelligence.
It's also about efficiency and sustainability.

Follow Harshit Harsh for simple Data Science & AI insights.
Repost or save this if it changed how you think about AI.
